publisher.publisher-name: The Korean Society of Veterinary Science

title-group.article-title: Pulmonary passage of canine adipose tissue-derived mesenchymal stem cells through intravenous transplantation in mouse model

article-meta.aff: 1Department of Veterinary Surgery, College of Veterinary Medicine, Konkuk University, Seoul 05029, Korea.
2Department of Small Animal Clinical Sciences, University of Florida College of Veterinary Medicine, Gainesville, FL 32611, USA.
3Department of Companion Animal Health, Yeonsung University, Anyang 14011, Korea.
4Department of Veterinary Obstetrics and Theriogenology, College of Veterinary Medicine, Konkuk University, Seoul 05029, Korea.

5Regenerative Medicine Laboratory, Center for Stem Cell Research, Department of Biomedical Science and Technology, Institute of Biomedical Science and Technology, School of Medicine, Konkuk University, Seoul 05029, Korea.
6KU Center for Animal Blood Medical Science, Konkuk University, Seoul 05029, Korea.

author-notes.corresp: Corresponding author: Hun-Young Yoon. Department of Veterinary Surgery, College of Veterinary Medicine, Konkuk University, 120 Neungdong-ro, Gwangjin-gu, Seoul 05029, Korea. yoonh@konkuk.ac.kr

permissions.copyright-statement: © 2024 The Korean Society of Veterinary Science
permissions.copyright-holder: The Korean Society of Veterinary Science

license.license-p: This is an Open Access article distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution Non-Commercial License (https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/4.0) which permits unrestricted non-commercial use, distribution, and reproduction in any medium, provided the original work is properly cited.

sec.title: Importance
sec.p: The intravenous administration of adipose tissue-derived mesenchymal stem cells (AdMSCs) in veterinary medicine is an attractive treatment option. On the other hand, it can result in severe complications, including pulmonary thromboembolism (PTE).

sec.title: Objective
sec.p: The present study assessed the occurrence of PTE after the intravenous infusion of canine AdMSCs (cAdMSCs) into experimental animals.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: Five-week-old male BALB/c hairless mice were categorized into groups labeled A to G. In the control group (A), fluorescently stained 2 × 106 cAdMSCs were diluted in 200 μL of suspension and injected into the tail vein as a single bolus. The remaining groups included the following: group B with 5 × 106 cells, group C with 3 × 106 cells, group D with 1 × 106 cells, group E with 1 × 106 cells injected twice with a one-day interval, group F with 2 × 106 cells in 100 μL of suspension, and group G with 2 × 106 cells in 300 μL of suspension.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: Group D achieved a 100% survival rate, while none of the subjects in groups B and C survived (p = 0.002). Blood tests revealed a tendency for the D-dimer levels to increase as the cell dose increased (p = 0.006). The platelet count was higher in the low cell concentration groups and lower in the high cell concentration groups (p = 0.028). A histological examination revealed PTE in most deceased subjects (96.30%).

sec.title: Conclusions and Relevance
sec.p: PTE was verified, and various variables were identified as potential contributing factors, including the cell dose, injection frequency, and suspension volume.

sec.title: INTRODUCTION
sec.p: Stem cell therapy has emerged as a prominent field in regenerative medicine, offering potential therapeutic applications. For stem cell therapy to be implemented effectively in clinical practice, adequate amounts of cells must be collected in a minimally invasive manner. They need to be differentiated into various cell types, and safe and efficient transplantation must be ensured. Adipose tissue-derived stem cells fulfill these criteria [1]. Among these stem cells, the intravenous transplantation of adipose tissue-derived mesenchymal stem cells (AdMSCs) has attracted attention as an appealing method in the field of cell-based treatments because of its simplicity of application, as well as the ease of extraction and cultivation [2].

In recent years, numerous studies have focused on the intravenous injection of mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs) in animals and humans [3]. Veterinary medicine, in particular, has undertaken a significant number of investigations on injecting adipose tissue-derived MSCs [3]. Intravenous injection studies of AdMSCs have been conducted in diverse areas of canine health, encompassing skin diseases, such as hepatocutaneous syndrome [4] and atopic dermatitis [5], digestive disorders like inflammatory bowel disease [6], and musculoskeletal conditions including skeletal muscle injury [7] and semitendinous myopathy [8]. In cats, intravenous infusion studies have been conducted in various areas such as acute/chronic kidney disease [910111213], feline chronic gingivostomatitis [1415], asthma [1617], and enteropathies [18].

The intravenous administration of AdMSCs has gained popularity as a regenerative medical treatment. On the other hand, it is essential to address potential serious side effects, such as the failure to pass through the pulmonary circulation before reaching the target organ and subsequent development of pulmonary thromboembolism (PTE), which can result in death [19202122]. Therefore, further research on complications is necessary to ensure the safe and effective clinical application of AdMSCs [19202122].

In the present study, the research involved observing MSCs in suspension and measuring their diameter. Moreover, the pulmonary passage of canine AdMSCs (cAdMSCs) after intravenous administration was investigated by observing the clinical manifestations during injection, monitoring the biodistribution using Spectral Ami HT, assessing the platelet and D-dimer levels, and confirming the PTE via histological examination of lung biopsies. Furthermore, the factors associated with pulmonary passage and the induction of pulmonary embolism were controlled, and the outcomes were juxtaposed by introducing cAdMSCs into experimental animals. In essence, this study evaluated the extent of pulmonary cell passage and the potential induction of pulmonary embolism by manipulating variables such as the cell dose, injection frequency, and suspension volume.

sec.title: Preparation of cAdMSCs
sec.p: cAdMSCs were cryopreserved, containing 5 × 105 – 5 × 106 cells in 1 mL of cell banker (Nippon Zenyaku Kogyo, Japan). Only cells within passage 3 were utilized for the experiment. The vial containing cryopreserved cAdMSCs was thawed at 37°C using a thermostatic water bath oscillator (Taitec Corporation, Japan). Subsequently, the cells were washed 2–3 times with full medium (complete stromal media), consisting of Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium/nutrient mixture F-12 (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA), supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (Tissue Culture Biologicals, USA) and 1% antibiotic-antimycotic (Thermo Fisher Scientific). The material was then centrifuged at 1,500 rpm for 5 min, and the supernatant was carefully discarded. The remaining cells were resuspended in full medium.

sec.title: Fluorescence staining of cAdMSCs
sec.p: Cells were suspended in phosphate-buffered saline (PBS; Biowest, France) at a density of 1 × 106 cells/mL. Next, 20 μL of CytoFlamma 749 cell membrane solution (Bioacts, Korea) per mL was added and thoroughly mixed by pipetting. The cells were incubated at 37°C for approximately 30 min, with agitation every ten min. Finally, the labeled cells were centrifuged at 1,300 rpm, 37°C, for 3 min.

sec.title: Cell size measurement
sec.p: The cells were suspended in PBS (Biowest) and observed using an inverted photomicroscope (Olympus Korea, Korea) at magnifications of ×100, ×200, and ×400. Images of the cells were captured and analyzed using ImageJ software (National Institutes of Health, USA). The maximum diameter of the cell cytoplasm was measured, and the average value was calculated from the collected data.

sec.title: Cell injection in mice
sec.p: Five-week-old male BALB/c hairless mice were obtained from DBL (Korea) for cell injection experiments. The mice were allowed one week to acclimatize to the laboratory environment, food pellets, and water. All animal-related procedures were conducted in accordance with the guidelines for animal use at Konkuk University, and the Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee (IACUC) approved this study (IACUC number KU17182-1).

For the cell injection procedure, the mice were placed in a restraining frame, and their tails were immersed in warm water for approximately five min to dilate the blood vessels. After disinfection with 70% alcohol, the fluorescence-dyed cAdMSCs were injected into the tail vein using a 0.5 mL insulin syringe (31 G) (Ultra-Fine II short needle; Becton-Dickinson and Company, USA) at a rate of 200 μL of PBS suspension per minute. After the injection, any bleeding at the injection site was controlled using gauze, and the mice were closely monitored.

sec.title: Control of variables (cell dose, injection frequency, and suspension volume)
sec.p: Cell doses of 5 × 106 cells, 3 × 106 cells, 2 × 106 cells, and 1 × 106 cells were diluted in 200 μL of PBS suspension to control the cell dose. These diluted cell suspensions were then injected at 200 μL per minute. In the experimental group where the injection frequency was adjusted, a cell dose of 1 × 106 cells was diluted in 200 μL of PBS suspension and injected at 200 μL per minute on day one. After 24 h, an additional injection of 1 × 106 cells was administered similarly. The experimental groups were designed to vary the suspension volume by diluting 2 × 106 cells in different amounts of PBS suspension. Specifically, 2 × 106 cells were diluted in 100 μL, 200 μL, and 300 μL of PBS suspension, and these diluted cell suspensions were injected at 200 μL per minute. Table 1 lists the classification of all experimental animal groups.

sec.title: In vitro, in vivo, and ex vivo fluorescence imaging for biodistribution analysis
sec.p: After cell injection, the mice were placed in the chamber of the Spectral Advanced Molecular Imaging (Ami) HTX (Spectral Instruments Imaging, USA) for fluorescence imaging. Supplementary Method 1 provides a detailed explanation.

sec.title: Post-injection monitoring related to PTE
sec.p: After cell inoculation, the experimental animals were monitored for clinical manifestations and survival. Blood samples were collected from the femoral vein to measure the CBC (platelet) and plasma D-dimer values after cell injection. In cases where animals died, blood was exsanguinated through the caudal vena cava for a histological examination, and blood samples were also collected. The CBC value was measured using the URIT-2900 VET Plus hematology analyzer (URIT Medical Electronics, China). The D-dimer levels in mice were measured using the Mouse D-Dimer (D2D) ELISA Kit (MBS269348; MyBioSource.com, USA) according to the protocol provided.

sec.title: Histology
sec.p: During the autopsy, the lungs were extracted and subjected to a biopsy. The comprehensive methods are elaborated in Supplementary Method 2.

sec.title: Statistical analysis
sec.p: All quantified data were analyzed using SPSS statistics software version 29.0 (IBM, USA). A p values < 0.05 were considered significant, and all experimental data are expressed as mean ± SD. The specific significance analysis methods are described in detail in Supplementary Method 3.

sec.title: Cell size measurement
sec.p: The diameters of MSC cytoplasm were measured in suspension. The mean diameter of the MSCs in suspension, determined from charged-coupled device camera images, ranged from 12.48 μm to 30.00 μm (17.06 ± 2.89, n = 50) (Fig. 1).

caption.title: MSCs size measurement. Cells were photographed at (A) × 100 magnification (scale bar = 100 µm), (B) × 200 (scale bar = 50 µm), and (C) × 400 (scale bar = 20 µm), respectively. The cell diameter was measured at × 400 magnification. (D) Bar graphs of MSCs cell diameter in suspension.
caption.p: MSCs, mesenchymal stem cells.

sec.title: Post-injection clinical manifestation and survival rate
sec.p: Most of the experimental animals that died in the present study experienced immediate or rapid mortality shortly after the injection. Some individuals exhibited symptoms such as convulsions, shivering, dyspnea, and tetraplegia. Table 2 and Supplementary Table 1 list the survival rates, with a visual representation in Supplementary Fig. 1.

caption.title: Survival rates of the experimental animals on day seven

table-wrap-foot.p: In the control group (A), 2 × 106 cAdMSCs were injected as a single bolus in 200 μL of suspension. Other groups included group B (5 × 106 cells), group C (3 × 106 cells), group D (1 × 106 cells), group E (1 × 106 cells injected twice with a one-day interval), group F (2 × 106 cells in 100 μL of suspension), and group G (2 × 106 cells in 300 μL of suspension), all compared to the control group.
cAdMSCs, canine adipose tissue-derived mesenchymal stem cells.

aIndicates a significant difference compared to the D group (p < 0.05); bIndicates a significant difference compared to the F group (p < 0.05). A p value lower than 0.05 indicates significant differences between the groups at a 95% confidence level.

sec.title: Post-injection blood tests (platelet, D-dimer)
sec.p: The average platelet count was lower in the experimental groups with high cell concentrations (groups B and C) but higher in the experimental groups with low cell concentrations (groups A and D) (p = 0.028). The average D-dimer level showed an increasing trend as the cell dose increased (p = 0.006). In pairwise comparisons of the D-dimer levels, there were significant differences between the following experimental groups: 1 × 106 cells – 3 × 106 cells (p = 0.037) and 1 × 106 cells – 5 × 106 cells (p = 0.022). In the group with double boluses (group E), the mean platelet count was slightly higher, and the mean D-dimer level was lower than the control group. On the other hand, there was no significant difference in platelet (p = 0.95) and D-dimer levels (p = 0.37). The mean platelet count increased as the suspension volume increased and the D-dimer level decreased. Nevertheless, no significant differences were found in the platelet (p = 0.61) and D-dimer levels (p = 0.68). Supplementary Tables 2 and 3 lists the platelet and D-dimer values for each experimental group and are illustrated as box-whisker plot graphs in Fig. 2.

caption.title: Box whisker plot graphs of platelet counts and D-dimer levels. (A) Platelet counts of the cell dose control experimental groups. (B) Platelet counts of the injection frequency control experimental groups. (C) Platelet counts of the suspension volume control experimental groups. (D) D-dimer levels of the cell dose control experimental groups. (E) D-dimer levels of the injection frequency control experimental groups. (F) D-dimer levels of the suspension volume control experimental groups. Each panel displays the maximum, upper quartile, IQR, median, lower quartile, and minimum values for the platelet counts and the D-dimer levels. Any deviation greater than 1.5 times the IQR is marked separately as an outlier.
caption.p: IQR, interquartile range.
*Indicates statistical significance between the two groups (p < 0.05). A p value lower than 0.05 indicates significant differences between the groups at a 95% confidence level.

sec.title: In vitro, in vivo, and ex vivo fluorescence imaging for biodistribution analysis
sec.p: Before the injection experiment, the in vitro fluorescence imaging experiment revealed an increase in fluorescence expression as the cell concentration increased (Fig. 3B). Statistical analysis of the in vivo region of interest (ROI) values was performed to compare and analyze the data from day one in each experimental group, while the data from day two, which completed the second injection, were compared in the double boluses experimental group (group E). In the cell dose-adjusted groups, pairwise comparisons of in vivo experiments revealed significant differences between the 1 × 106 cells – 2 × 106 cells (p = 0.008), 1 × 106 cells – 5 × 106 cells (p = 0.00), and 3 × 106 cells – 5 × 106 cells (p = 0.007) experimental groups. The average ROI values were slightly larger in Group E than in the control group, but the significance of the ROI value was not found (p = 0.60). Similarly, no significant differences in ROI values were identified among the experimental groups with different suspension volumes (p = 0.12). Supplementary Tables 4, 5, and 6 show the ROI values for each day in the experimental groups with the cell dose, frequency, and suspension volume control, presented as mean ± SD. Supplementary Figs. 2 and 3C present in vivo fluorescence imaging figures, while Supplementary Fig. 3 offers a graph illustrating all ROI numerical data and mean values.

caption.title: Fluorescence imaging. (A) In vitro fluorescence imaging of varying numbers of MSCs. (B) Linear relationship between the cell numbers and p/s in in vitro fluorescence imaging. (C) In vivo fluorescence imaging. Image showing how to set a pulmonary region as a ROI on an in vivo scan. (D) Ex vivo fluorescence imaging. The numbers 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, and 6 represent the following organs: heart, lung, liver, spleen, kidneys, and intestines, respectively. The negative control is an object injected with saline instead of MSCs. The bar of the pseudo-color image indicates ranges of light intensity levels.
caption.p: p/s, photon counts per second; ROI, region of interest; MSCs, mesenchymal stem cells.

sec.p: Ex vivo imaging revealed fluorescence only in the lungs of the individuals that died after the injection, and no organs other than the lungs showed fluorescence. In the case of Group E, however, the second injection was made with the dead object already distributed in organs other than the lungs. During the week-long experiment, fluorescence was observed in organs other than the lungs in all surviving individuals. Among the experimental groups injected with 2 × 106 cells, fluorescence was observed in the liver, including the lungs, in the surviving individuals. In contrast, when injected with 1 × 106 cells, fluorescence was observed in the liver, spleen, and intestines on day seven. All ex vivo ROI data are presented in Supplementary Table 7 and visually in Fig. 3D and Supplementary Fig. 4.

sec.title: Histology
sec.p: Based on a histological examination, PTE was confirmed in 96.30% (26/27) of the deceased subjects. The histological findings of individuals with confirmed PTE revealed fibrin pigmentation within pulmonary vessels and plaque formation. In the cell-dose control groups, a significant difference in the PTE grade was observed among the groups based on the changes in cell concentration (p = 0.003). Pairwise comparisons revealed the following significant differences between experimental groups: between 1 × 106 cells and 5 × 106 cells (p = 0.023) and between 1 × 106 cells and 3 × 106 cells (p = 0.005). The proportion of subjects with confirmed PTE tended to increase as the cell concentration increased, and the ratio was smaller in the group with adjusted injection frequency (group E) compared to the control group. On the other hand, there was no significant difference in PTE grade between the control group and group E (p = 0.84). Furthermore, in the experimental group with the adjusted suspension volume, there was a decrease in the average PTE grade (p = 0.044), and the percentage of confirmed PTE tended to decrease as the volume increased. Supplementary Table 8 presents the PTE grades, and Supplementary Table 9 indicates the percentage of identified PTE. Fig. 4A-F visually compares the biopsy images with normal findings and those displaying PTE, while Fig. 4G graphically represents PTE grade and ratio.

caption.title: Photomicrographs of lung tissue: normal or PTE findings and graphs of PTE grades and ratio. (A) Normal (scale bar = 100 µm). (B) Normal (scale bar = 50 µm). (C) Normal (scale bar = 100 µm). (D) PTE. Prominent endothelium (dashed yellow line), adventitial inflammation (arrowheads), and initial plaque (• dotted circle) are identified in the PTE sample (scale bar = 100 µm). (E) PTE. Amorphous round fibrin-like materials in the vessels (arrow) are confirmed (scale bar = 100 µm). (F) PTE. Amorphous round fibrin-like materials in the vessels (arrows) are confirmed (scale bar = 50 µm). (A-F) Hematoxylin and eosin staining. (G) Graphs of the PTE grades and ratio. In the control group (A), 2 × 106 cAdMSCs were injected as a single bolus in 200 μL of suspension. Other groups included group B (5 × 106 cells), group C (3 × 106 cells), group D (1 × 106 cells), group E (1 × 106 cells injected twice with a one-day interval), group F (2 × 106 cells in 100 μL of suspension), and group G (2 × 106 cells in 300 μL of suspension), all compared to the control group.
caption.p: The classification criteria for PTE grades are as follows: grade 0 (normal/thromboembolism confirmed in < 10% of the vessels); grade 1 (low/thromboembolism confirmed in 10%–30% of the vessels); grade 2 (mid/thromboembolism confirmed in 30%–60% of the vessels); grade 3 (high/thromboembolism confirmed in > 60% of the vessels).
PTE, pulmonary thromboembolism; cAdMSCs, canine adipose tissue-derived mesenchymal stem cells.

sec.title: DISCUSSION
sec.p: Adverse events that may occur during an intravascular injection of MSCs include the heterogeneity of MSCs, potential tumor formation, imprecise organ targeting, and thrombosis and embolism formation. Among these, pulmonary embolism has consistently emerged as a significant concern, particularly in intravenous infusion [19202122]. The present study also confirmed PTE in most of the deceased subjects (96.30%), underscoring its role as a significant cause of fatalities during intravenous stem cell injections. While numerous studies have examined intravenous injections of bone marrow-derived stem cells, research on adipose tissue-derived stem cells is relatively limited. Most studies applied human AdMSCs to mouse or human models or mouse AdMSCs to mouse models [19202122]. Veterinary medicine research has focused on efficacy, with limited studies into the PTE-related side effects.

After intravenous injection of MSCs, rapid lung entrapment occurs due to the instant-blood-mediated inflammatory reaction (IBMIR) linked to thrombosis. This phenomenon arises when MSCs encounter blood, leading to tissue factor (TF) expression initiating clot formation. Various studies have reported coagulation activation post-cell injection [2425]. Platelet levels decrease after MSC injection, followed by an increase in the D-dimer levels, a coagulation marker, signifying platelet depletion and dimer formation due to coagulation [252627]. In particular, in the present study, the average platelet count in the negative control group (injected with normal saline – 0.9% NS, JW Pharmaceutical, Korea) (169.33 ± 39.51 109/L, n = 3) exceeded that of the entire experimental group (131.98 ± 48.23 109/L, n = 43). Furthermore, the average D-dimer value in the experimental group, particularly at higher cell doses, revealed an increase, indirectly supporting this observation. Furthermore, the accumulation of a substantial number of injected cells in the lung was attributed to interactions between the lung tissue and MSCs. Adhesion molecules like alpha4beta1/vascular cell adhesion molecule (VCAM)-1 have been reported to facilitate the association between MSCs and lung endothelial cells [2829]. Fischer et al. [28] in 2009 examined lung passage after pretreatment with various adhesion antibodies and confirmed the increased lung passage of MSCs upon deactivation of VCAM-1. This study showed that receptor-mediated adhesion expression and cell size play a crucial role in the pulmonary passage of MSCs [28].

Biodistribution studies of MSCs have been conducted, which generally reported that, following intravenous injection in animal models, most cells initially accumulate in the lung capillary filter before being distributed to other organs such as the liver and spleen [30]. These findings were also confirmed in the present experiment. Across all experimental groups in ex vivo imaging, except for the double boluses experimental group, cells were exclusively found in the lungs of deceased individuals, while organs other than the lung revealed a cell presence in surviving individuals during the week-long experiment. These findings suggest that the entrapment of cells in the lung is the cause of death in the experimental subjects. On the other hand, the in vivo ROI value did not show complete consistency with quantitative analysis based on the cell concentration or suspension volume. This discrepancy may be due to the selection of the lung as the ROI estimation site and the limitations associated with in vivo analysis. The accuracy of in vivo analysis may be lower than ex vivo analysis, where organs are directly extracted and analyzed. This limitation should be considered when interpreting the results.

Several studies have consistently pointed to the larger size of MSCs compared to pulmonary capillaries as a primary factor contributing to PTE [192831]. The size of MSCs, ranging from approximately 15 to 30 μm, exceeds the dimensions of capillaries in various animal species, including humans, as consistently reported in the relevant literature and confirmed by measurements in the present experiment [192831]. Consequently, most intravenously injected stem cells become trapped in the lungs, which aligns with the present study findings. Addressing this issue necessitates either reducing the diameter of MSCs or increasing the size of the pulmonary capillaries, both of which pose practical challenges. Schrepfer et al. [31] reported that the administration of sodium nitroprusside, a vasodilator, led to an increased number of cells crossing the lung barrier, suggesting the potential use of vasodilators as an option. In addition, Wang et al. [32] reported that relatively larger MSCs tend to accumulate in the lung capillaries. Therefore, using a strainer to select smaller MSCs or making efforts to culture MSCs with a reduced size uniformly could be viable approaches, albeit challenging [32].

The capacity of the cells plays a crucial role in determining the occurrence of PTE. Reducing the number of cells administered can decrease the incidence of PTE, but finding the appropriate cell concentration that is both effective and devoid of side effects is essential. The effective dose may vary according to the disease, treatment objectives, and target organ, making establishing a specific concentration threshold for effectiveness challenging. Furthermore, Ra et al. [21] observed no side effects during a 13-week observation period in SCID mice injected intravenously with human adipose tissue-derived MSCs at a maximum concentration of 2.5 × 108 cells/kg body weight. Complications increase as the cell concentration increases, both in human and veterinary medicine, and high concentrations of MSCs during intravascular injection have been associated with thrombosis [113334]. Several studies examined complications associated with the cell concentration, and current guidelines commonly used in human medicine recommend MSC concentrations ranging from 1 to 10 × 106 cells/kg. In mouse studies, the concentration is typically 4–5 times higher than in humans [35]. In the current experiment, the highest concentration of 5 × 106 cells/mouse was administered, which exceeds the range used in previous studies, considering the weight of a 20 g mouse. Achieving a 100% survival rate at a concentration of 1 × 106 cells/mouse reaffirms the validity of the aforementioned guidelines. During the experimental design process, the standard concentration was determined based on the cited papers, and a preliminary pilot experiment helped establish a lethal dose at a lower concentration of 2 × 106 cells/20 g.

The dose and injection speed have been identified as factors affecting the occurrence of embolisms during the systemic injection of MSCs [33637]. Several studies have highlighted the importance of a slow injection to prevent the undesirable consequences of excessive cell accumulation in pulmonary blood vessels, particularly when administering high doses of MSCs intravascularly [212238]. Slower injection rates led to reduced cell damage and inhibited the expression of procoagulant TF (TF/CD142), which is associated with the IBMIR [3536]. The injection rate standard is typically defined as the amount of solution administered per hour, and there is variation in this parameter across studies. While some studies referenced the number of cells injected per hour as a parameter [38], most research suggests that the injection rate should be slow, with the ultimate goal being to minimize cell trapping. All experimental results of this study related to suspension volume supported the idea that the number of cells infused per hour, or the injection rate, significantly influenced the occurrence of PTE.

In the current experiment, the infusion interval in the experimental group that received two injections of 1 × 106 cells was set at 24 h between injections. This choice was made based on the understanding that IBMIR, a known mechanism for inducing PTE, typically occurs as an immediate reaction within 24 h of the cell injection [35]. The frequency of infusion has been explored in relation to pulmonary passage, and Fischer et al. [28] reported that double bolus infusions increased pulmonary passage compared to a single infusion. Hence, the first injection saturates receptors and eases intravascular passage during the second injection [28]. These results suggest that increasing the frequency of injections reduces the procoagulant activity of MSCs. Several studies reported that injecting multiple intravascular MSCs in humans and veterinary applications were well-tolerated and had therapeutic effects [3940]. Specifically, when administering high-dose MSCs intravenously, dividing the dose into smaller injections at regular intervals can be a strategy to induce therapeutic effects while maintaining a higher level of safety.

In conclusion, this study examined PTE in mice after the intravenous administration of MSCs from dog adipose tissue. PTE was confirmed in most deceased cases, highlighting its significance as a leading cause of fatality during stem cell intravenous injections. The larger diameter of MSCs compared to the lung capillaries was identified as a potential contributor. Various factors influencing PTE were explored, leading to recommendations for risk reduction. Strategies include lowering the cell concentrations, increasing injection frequency with a constant total cell count, adopting a slower injection rate, and using larger suspension volumes to decrease the cell influx per hour. These approaches aim to minimize PTE and enhance the safety of stem cell intravenous administration. This study offers valuable insights for developing clinical protocols for the intravenous administration of MSCs derived from adipose tissue in veterinary contexts, especially in dogs. Further research and clinical trials will be needed to validate and refine these strategies for safer and more effective stem cell therapies.

fn.p: Conflict of Interest: The authors declare no conflicts of interest.

list-item.p: Data curation: Kwon J, Kim MY.
Formal analysis: Kwon J, Kim MY.
Project administration: Lee S, Lee J, Yoon HY.
Writing - review & editing: Kwon J, Yoon HY.

caption.p: In vitro, in vivo and ex vivo fluorescence imaging for biodistribution analysis

caption.p: Histology

caption.p: Statistical analysis

caption.title: Supplementary Table 1
caption.p: Statistical differences in survival rates between the experimental groups

caption.title: Supplementary Table 2
caption.p: Platelet counts (109/L) of experimental animals

caption.title: Supplementary Table 3
caption.p: D-dimer levels (ng/ml) of experimental animals

caption.title: Supplementary Table 4
caption.p: In vivo fluorescence ROI values ([×107] p/s) of the cell dose control experimental groups

caption.title: Supplementary Table 5
caption.p: In vivo fluorescence ROI values ([×107] p/s) of the injection frequency control experimental groups

caption.title: Supplementary Table 6
caption.p: In vivo fluorescence ROI values ([×107] p/s) of the suspension volume control experimental groups

caption.title: Supplementary Table 7
caption.p: Ex vivo fluorescence ROI values ([×107] p/s) of the experimental animals

caption.title: Supplementary Table 8
caption.p: PTE grade confirmed by biopsy of lung tissue of the experimental animals

caption.title: Supplementary Table 9
caption.p: Ratio of PTE confirmed by biopsy

caption.title: Supplementary Fig. 1
caption.p: Bar graphs of survival rates on day seven of experimental animals.

caption.title: Supplementary Fig. 2
caption.p: In vivo fluorescence imaging. (A) In vivo fluorescence imaging according to the cell dose control on day one. (B) In vivo fluorescence imaging by the date of experimental animals Injected with 1 × 106 cells. The bar of the pseudo-color image indicates ranges of light intensity levels.

caption.title: Supplementary Fig. 3
caption.p: Time course graphs of in vivo fluorescence ROI values. (A) Time course graphs of the in vivo fluorescence ROI values of the cell dose control experimental groups. (B) Time course graphs of the in vivo fluorescence ROI values of the injection frequency control experimental groups. (C) Time course graphs of the in vivo fluorescence ROI values of the suspension volume control experimental groups. All ROI values of the experimental groups by dates are shown in the figures, and the average values are indicated by a line. Each figure displays the ROI values of the front and back measured in one object, respectively. That means there were two measurements per individual (front/back). The measured values per object are all expressed in the same figure. In other words, the front and back are marked as one figure without any difference.

caption.title: Supplementary Fig. 4
caption.p: Bar graphs of ex vivo fluorescence ROI values. (A) Ex vivo ROI values by organ for each experimental group on day one. However, E (two boluses/dead) represents the data from day two when the second injection was completed. (B) Ex vivo ROI values by organ for each experimental group on day seven. (C) Ex vivo ROI values by organ and week after 1 × 106 cell injection. The data from weeks two, three, and four indicate the fluorescence imaging results of the additional pilot study. The “live/dead” column indicates the condition (survival) of the experimental animal at the time of measurement.
